---
title: "Session 1 — Exercise Solution"
subtitle: "Setting Up a Reproducible Workflow"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

## Introduction

This report examines the relationship between economic development and health
outcomes across countries. Understanding this relationship is relevant for
international businesses assessing market conditions and risk in emerging
economies.

As discussed in @BekezKezdi2021, regression analysis allows us to estimate the
average association between two variables while holding other factors constant.
For a formal econometric treatment of these methods, see @Wooldridge2020.

## Data

The analysis uses data from the World Bank covering 182 countries in 2017.
The key variables are GDP per capita (in USD) and life expectancy at birth
(in years). The foundational empirical relationship between income and health
was documented by @Preston1975.

## Results

The main finding from the literature is that richer countries tend to have
higher life expectancy — but the relationship is non-linear: gains in life
expectancy per additional dollar of income shrink as countries become wealthier.
A log transformation of GDP per capita helps capture this pattern.

```{r}
#| label: fig-example
#| fig-cap: "Engine displacement and fuel economy by vehicle class (mpg dataset)."

library(tidyverse)

ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = class)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    x = "Engine displacement (litres)",
    y = "Highway fuel economy (mpg)",
    colour = "Vehicle class"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## References
